nodes.name: Element
nodes.summary: Abstract base class for all electrical schematic symbols in QET. Manages terminals, conductor connectivity, dynamic text items, element text groups, XML persistence, element-linking (master/slave/report), and defines the kind enum (Simple, Master, Slave, NextReport, PreviousReport, Terminale). All concrete element types (SimpleElement, MasterElement, SlaveElement, ReportElement) inherit from this class.

edges.label: uses to build elements for cache population

layers.name: Application Shell
layers.description: Top-level Qt application singleton (QETApp), main window, diagram editor window, project/diagram view controllers, and user-facing export and print dialogs that orchestrate the full QElectroTech session lifecycle.

layers.name: Diagram Core
layers.description: Central domain model for QET projects and diagrams: the QGraphicsScene-based Diagram, QetProject file model, conductor geometry (segments and profiles), titleblock cell model, border/titleblock binding, diagram event handlers, and undo commands that mutate diagram state.

layers.name: Graphics Items
layers.description: All QGraphicsItem subclasses rendered on the diagram canvas — elements (master, slave, simple, terminal, report), conductors, text items, cross-references, images, shapes, table view items, and the generic drag handle modeler used during interactive editing.

layers.name: Element Editor
layers.description: Standalone graphical editor for authoring QET element symbols: the QGraphicsScene-based element scene, all primitive graphics parts (arc, ellipse, line, polygon, rectangle, terminal, text, dynamic field), editor event handlers, editor undo commands, and the element editor main window UI.

layers.name: Domain Features
layers.description: Vertical feature modules implementing IEC 60617 domain concepts: the terminal strip editor (TerminalStrip) with its graphics items and undo commands, the conductor/element auto-numbering engine (autoNum), the titleblock template system (titleblock), the XML element library browser (ElementsCollection), the SQLite project database (dataBase), the cross-folio search-and-replace tool (SearchAndReplace), the DXF-to-element importer, and the element scaler utility.

layers.name: UI Forms & Dialogs
layers.description: Qt Designer .ui form files and their paired C++ dialog/widget classes for conductor, element, text, image, and titleblock properties editors; the generic PropertiesEditor dock framework; rich-text editing widgets; and all context/configuration panel widgets embedded in the main application.

layers.name: Shared Utilities
layers.description: Cross-cutting support code reused across features: the multi-language NamesList type, property data types (ElementData, TerminalData, XrefProperties), element and table factory classes, QUndoCommand infrastructure (QPropertyUndoCommand), XML/SVG serialization helpers, icon and messagebox wrappers (qeticons, qetmessagebox, qetxml), and small utility widgets (QWidgetAnimation, text-orientation spinbox).

layers.name: Project Support
layers.description: All non-C++ project infrastructure: GitHub Actions CI pipelines, CMake build modules, test stubs (Catch2/GTest/GMock/QtTest), Qt translation source files (lang/), application icons (ico/), fonts, license texts, Unix man pages, platform packaging scripts (Flatpak, Snap, AppImage, Windows NSIS), example .qet project files, and project-level documentation (README, CONTRIBUTING, ChangeLog).

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: The README introduces QElectroTech as a libre CAD/CAE desktop application for creating IEC 60617 wiring diagrams, terminal strips, and multi-folio electrical schematics. It immediately sets scope: no simulation or calculation — this is a pure schematic documentation tool. Reading it first establishes the vocabulary (elements, conductors, folios, titleblocks) that the rest of the codebase uses consistently. The technical stack is Qt6/C++, KF6, CMake, and SQLite, all of which you will encounter in the source.
tour.languageLesson: QET uses the term 'folio' (from French engineering practice) for a single diagram sheet within a multi-page project. This is distinct from a 'diagram' (the data model) and a 'view' (the rendered widget) — three separate concepts that map to three distinct classes in the codebase.

tour.title: Application Entry Point
tour.description: main.cpp is the literal starting point of the QElectroTech process. It configures SingleApplication to prevent duplicate instances, installs a custom Qt message handler that writes timestamped log entries, and then calls QETApp to start the event loop. This file is deliberately thin — its only job is to assemble the process-level infrastructure and hand off to the application singleton. Understanding it shows you what QET requires before it can show its first window.
tour.languageLesson: SingleApplication is a drop-in QApplication replacement that uses shared memory and a local socket to detect and forward activation to an already-running instance. QET uses this to ensure only one process manages the same project file at a time.

tour.title: Application Singleton: QETApp
tour.description: QETApp is the nerve centre of the running process — a QApplication subclass that owns every global resource: the element and titleblock collections, the list of open projects, the recent-files list, singleton factories, and the single QUndoGroup spanning all open projects. Any code anywhere in QET that needs a shared resource calls QETApp::instance() to get it. The header's fan-in count of 76 (second highest in the codebase) reflects this: it is included by almost every subsystem. Studying qetapp.h and qetapp.cpp teaches you what the global state of QET actually is.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's QApplication subclassing pattern lets you attach application-wide services to the QApplication object itself. This avoids raw global variables while still providing a single, process-lifetime-managed home for shared resources like icon caches and open-document lists.

tour.title: Global Namespace and Enums: qet.h
tour.description: qet.h declares the QET utility namespace — a collection of enums (Orientation, EndType, DiagramArea), free functions, and small helpers shared across every layer of the application. With a fan-in of 21, it is included nearly everywhere. This file is the project's common vocabulary: before reading any domain class, understanding qet.h tells you the base types everything else builds on.
tour.languageLesson: Grouping project-wide enums and utility functions in a dedicated namespace header (rather than a class) is idiomatic Qt/C++. It avoids artificial class coupling while still giving all consumers a single, stable include path.

tour.title: Project Model: QETProject
tour.description: QETProject is the top-level document model — the in-memory representation of a .qet file. It owns an ordered list of Diagram objects (the folios), embedded element and titleblock collections stored as XML DOM trees, terminal strips, and auto-numbering configuration. It handles project-level XML serialisation and emits signals when diagrams are added or removed. With a fan-in of 41, it is the fourth most-imported header in the codebase, touched by the main window, every project view, the database layer, and the export pipeline.
tour.languageLesson: QET projects are stored as XML, with embedded element definitions living inside the same .qet file. XmlElementCollection (a QDomDocument wrapper) provides the in-memory store for these embedded elements, letting a project be fully self-contained as a single file.

tour.title: Diagram: The Core Scene Model
tour.description: Diagram is a QGraphicsScene subclass and the central domain object for a single folio. It owns all the QGraphicsItem instances — elements, conductors, text items, shapes — and is responsible for their XML serialisation and deserialisation. With a fan-in of 88 (the highest in the entire codebase), diagram.h is the single most widely depended-upon header: the view, undo commands, export dialog, terminal strip, and nearly every feature module all include it. Understanding Diagram means understanding the container for everything drawn on screen.
tour.languageLesson: QGraphicsScene stores items in a spatial index and manages their coordinate system, signals, and painting order. QET subclasses it to add domain-specific ownership (elements know their Diagram), XML round-trip, and clipboard/undo integration. The scene does not paint itself — that is the job of the paired QGraphicsView.

tour.title: Diagram View and Main Window
tour.description: DiagramView is the QGraphicsView that renders one Diagram folio and handles user input — drag-and-drop of elements from the panel, zoom, scroll, and delegation to the active DiagramEventInterface editing mode. ProjectView wraps multiple DiagramViews in a tabbed widget, one per folio. QETDiagramEditor is the main application window (MDI host) that holds multiple ProjectViews, wires all menus and toolbars, and orchestrates project-level operations like save, export, and print. These three classes form the entire visible shell of QET.

tour.title: Graphics Items: Elements, Conductors, Terminals
tour.description: The qetgraphicsitem/ directory contains all the QGraphicsItem subclasses that populate a Diagram. Element is the base class for all schematic symbols (fan-in 56 — third highest); Terminal is the attachment point where a conductor connects to an element; Conductor is the wire or bus drawn between two Terminals. MasterElement extends Element for coil-type symbols that own a cross-reference and link to slave contacts, embodying the IEC 60617 master/slave convention. DynamicElementTextItem provides formula-driven annotation text that auto-updates from element properties.
tour.languageLesson: QET's master/slave element model maps directly to IEC 60617: a master element (e.g., a contactor coil) declares its cross-reference, and slave elements (e.g., NO/NC contacts) are linked to it. The cross-reference item on the diagram is automatically updated as links are added or removed.

tour.title: Interactive Editing: Event Strategy Pattern
tour.description: DiagramEventInterface is the abstract strategy for all interactive diagram editing modes. When the user selects 'Add element', 'Draw shape', or 'Add text', DiagramView installs the appropriate concrete DiagramEvent subclass (DiagramEventAddElement, DiagramEventAddShape, DiagramEventAddText) which then intercepts all mouse and keyboard events until the operation completes or is cancelled. This strategy pattern keeps the DiagramView free of mode-specific logic while making it easy to add new editing modes without touching existing code.
tour.languageLesson: The Strategy pattern in C++ is commonly implemented with a pure-virtual base class and a raw or smart pointer to the active strategy. QET's DiagramEventInterface uses Qt's event system: the active strategy receives QMouseEvent/QKeyEvent calls directly, which is cleaner than a large switch statement inside DiagramView.

tour.title: Undo/Redo Infrastructure
tour.description: QET provides fully undoable editing through two complementary mechanisms. QPropertyUndoCommand is a generic QUndoCommand subclass that stores before/after values for any Q_PROPERTY and applies them via Qt's property system — used for simple property changes on any QObject. diagramcommands.h declares a family of specialised commands (PasteDiagramCommand, CutDiagramCommand, MoveElementsCommand) for structural diagram operations. deleteqgraphicsitemcommand.h handles the common case of deleting one or more graphics items. Together they form the complete mutation surface of the diagram model.
tour.languageLesson: Qt's QUndoStack/QUndoCommand pattern requires every state mutation to be encapsulated as a command object with redo() and undo() methods. QET groups all open projects under a single QUndoGroup in QETApp, so the active undo stack automatically switches when the user changes the focused project window.

tour.title: Element Editor
tour.description: QET ships a standalone symbol editor for authoring the XML element files (.elmt) that populate the schematic library. QETElementEditor is the editor's main window. ElementScene is its QGraphicsScene, managing all the drawing primitives (arcs, ellipses, lines, polygons, rectangles, terminals, text, dynamic fields). When the user saves, ElementScene serialises the scene to the standard QET element XML format. This editor is effectively a mini-application embedded within QET, sharing QETApp globals but with its own undo stack and toolbar.
tour.languageLesson: QET element files (.elmt) are XML documents describing graphical primitives in a normalised coordinate space. The same XML schema that the element editor writes is what the diagram loader reads via ElementFactory — a single format serving both authoring and runtime.

tour.title: Element Library and Factory
tour.description: The ElementsCollection subsystem manages the three-tiered element library: the common (built-in) collection on disk, the user's custom collection, and elements embedded inside a specific .qet project. ElementsCollectionWidget renders all three as a unified tree in the dock panel. ElementsTreeView enables drag-and-drop from the tree onto the diagram canvas. ElementFactory is the thread-safe singleton that, given an ElementsLocation, constructs the correct Element subclass (MasterElement, SlaveElement, SimpleElement, etc.). This pipeline — browser → location → factory → graphics item — is how every element reaches the diagram.

tour.title: Auto-Numbering and Terminal Strips
tour.description: Two major domain features sit in their own directories. The autoNum subsystem assigns sequential reference numbers to elements and conductors using configurable NumerotationContext sequences (e.g., %id-%row%col patterns). The TerminalStrip subsystem models physical terminal strips (e.g., Phoenix Contact rail blocks) as ordered PhysicalTerminal/RealTerminal objects, generates table-form diagrams, and has its own undo commands and graphical editor. Both subsystems interact with QETProject for persistence and with the diagram layer for visual feedback, illustrating how QET's vertical feature modules integrate with the core.
tour.languageLesson: NumerotationContext uses a value-type design (copyable, XML-serialisable) so that numbering sequences can be stored per-project and per-conductor-type without reference-counting complexity. This contrasts with TerminalStrip, which is a QObject (reference semantics) because it manages live connections to diagram items.

tour.title: XML Serialisation and Project Database
tour.description: The QETXML namespace (qetxml.h/cpp) provides the canonical helpers for reading and writing QPen, QBrush, QMargins, and all diagram item types to/from QDomElement. It is included by 19 files and forms the serialisation backbone of the .qet file format. Alongside it, projectDataBase maintains an in-memory SQLite mirror of the project's elements and diagram metadata, enabling fast SQL-based queries for the cross-folio search-and-replace tool and the BOM export dialog — avoiding repeated XML tree traversals for reporting operations.
tour.languageLesson: QET's dual-representation pattern — XML on disk (for portability and diff-friendliness) plus an in-memory SQLite database (for fast querying) — is a pragmatic engineering tradeoff. The SQLite mirror is rebuilt from XML every time the project opens and is never persisted, so the XML file remains the single source of truth.

tour.title: Build System and CI
tour.description: CMakeLists.txt is the root build file that ties everything together: it links Qt6, KF6, SQLite3, pugixml, and the SingleApplication submodule, compiles the translation files via Qt's lupdate/lrelease pipeline, and configures platform-specific install paths. Two GitHub Actions workflows complete the picture — auto-doxygen.yml generates and deploys API documentation on every tagged release, while windows-build.yml validates the Windows (MSYS2/Qt5) build on every push to master. Reading these files shows what the project's dependencies are, how it is released, and where to look when the CI turns red.
tour.languageLesson: CMake's FetchContent module (used here for KF6) downloads and builds dependencies at configure time, keeping them version-pinned in the build tree without requiring system-wide installation. This is how the Qt6 port branch tracks KF6 independently of whatever KDE version the system has installed.
